chapter_info.subject: Mathematics
chapter_info.chapter_name: A Tale of Three Intersecting Lines

content.text: A triangle is the most basic closed shape formed by three line segments. Every triangle consists of three essential components: three corner points called vertices, and three line segments called sides that join pairs of vertices.

content.title: Triangle Notation
content.text: A triangle is denoted using the symbol ∆ followed by the names of its three vertices. For example, ∆ABC represents a triangle with vertices A, B, and C. The vertices can be named in any order.

The three sides meeting at each corner create three angles. In ∆ABC, the angles are ∠CAB (angle at A), ∠ABC (angle at B), and ∠BCA (angle at C), which are simply denoted as ∠A, ∠B, and ∠C respectively.
Triangles come in various shapes and sizes, creating a rich diversity of geometric forms. Understanding these different types and their properties is fundamental to geometry.

topics.topic_name: Definition and Properties of Equilateral Triangles

content.text: An equilateral triangle is a special triangle in which all three sides have equal length. Among all triangles, equilateral triangles possess the highest symmetry.

content.title: Key Property
content.text: In an equilateral triangle, all sides are equal. If one side has length a, then all three sides have length a. For example, in an equilateral triangle with side length 4 cm, all three sides are exactly 4 cm.

topics.topic_name: Construction of Equilateral Triangles Using a Compass

content.text: Constructing an equilateral triangle using only a ruler is possible but requires many trials. Using a compass makes the construction more efficient and accurate.

content.title: Constructing an Equilateral Triangle with Side 4 cm
content.text:
Step 1: Draw the base AB = 4 cm using a ruler.
Step 2: Using a compass with radius 4 cm, draw an arc from point A. The third vertex C must lie on this arc.
Step 3: Using the same radius (4 cm), draw another arc from point B. This arc must intersect the first arc.
Step 4: Mark the intersection point as C. Since C lies on both arcs, AC = 4 cm and BC = 4 cm.
Step 5: Join AC and BC to complete the equilateral triangle ABC.

content.title: Why This Works
content.text: The construction ensures that both AC and BC are of length 4 cm because C is the intersection of two circles: one centered at A with radius 4 cm, and another centered at B with radius 4 cm. Any point on a circle is at a fixed distance (the radius) from the center.

notes.section_title: 7.2 Constructing Triangles When All Sides are Given

topics.topic_name: General Construction Method for Given Side Lengths

content.text: When three side lengths are given (not necessarily equal), we can construct a triangle using the same compass method, with a modification to accommodate different lengths.

content.title: Constructing a Triangle with Sides 4 cm, 5 cm, and 6 cm
content.text:
Step 1: Choose one side as the base. Draw AB = 4 cm (the shortest side).
Step 2: From point A, construct an arc with radius 5 cm (the length of side AC). A and C points will be 5 cm apart.
Step 3: From point B, construct an arc with radius 6 cm (the length of side BC). B and C points will be 6 cm apart.
Step 4: The point where both arcs intersect is the required vertex C. Join AC and BC to complete ∆ABC.

The key insight is that we need to find a point C that is simultaneously at distance 5 cm from A and distance 6 cm from B. This point is the intersection of two circles centered at A and B with respective radii.

Not all sets of three lengths can form a triangle. For example, lengths 3 cm, 4 cm, and 8 cm cannot form a triangle. There is a condition that determines whether a triangle exists for a given set of lengths.

content.title: Triangle Inequality Rule
content.text:
A set of three lengths can form a triangle if and only if each length is smaller than the sum of the other two lengths. Mathematically, for lengths a, b, and c:
- a < b + c
- b < a + c
- c < a + b

content.title: Checking If Lengths Form a Triangle
content.text:
For lengths 4 cm, 5 cm, 8 cm:
- 4 < 5 + 8 = 13 ✓
- 5 < 4 + 8 = 12 ✓
- 8 < 4 + 5 = 9 ✓
All three conditions are satisfied, so a triangle exists.

For lengths 3 cm, 4 cm, 8 cm:
- 3 < 4 + 8 = 12 ✓
- 4 < 3 + 8 = 11 ✓
- 8 < 3 + 4 = 7 ✗ (This fails!)
Since one condition fails, no triangle exists.

The intuition behind the triangle inequality comes from the shortest path principle: the direct path between two points is always shorter than any roundabout path. If one side of a triangle were greater than or equal to the sum of the other two, the triangle could not close.
Triangles are classified by their sides into three types based on the number of equal sides.

content.title: Classification Based on Sides
items: Equilateral triangle: All three sides are equal length.
Isosceles triangle: Two sides are equal length.
Scalene triangle: All three sides have different lengths.

content.title: Key Observation
content.text: An equilateral triangle is a special case of an isosceles triangle where not just two but all three sides are equal.

notes.section_title: 7.3 Constructing Triangles with Sides and Angles

topics.topic_name: Construction with Two Sides and Included Angle (SAS)

content.text: An important construction method uses two known side lengths and the angle between them (called the included angle). This method is denoted SAS (Side-Angle-Side).

content.title: Constructing a Triangle with AB = 5 cm, AC = 4 cm, and ∠A = 45°
content.text:
Step 1: Draw the base AB = 5 cm using a ruler.
Step 2: At point A, construct an angle of 45° using a protractor. Draw the arm of this angle.
Step 3: On this arm, mark point C such that AC = 4 cm.
Step 4: Join BC to complete the triangle ABC.

For any combination of two sides and an included angle (where the angle is less than 180°), a unique triangle can always be constructed. This is because the two sides and the angle between them completely determine the shape and size of the triangle.

topics.topic_name: Construction with Two Angles and Included Side (ASA)

content.text: Another construction method uses two known angles and the side between them (the included side). This method is denoted ASA (Angle-Side-Angle).

content.title: Constructing a Triangle with AB = 5 cm, ∠A = 45°, and ∠B = 80°
content.text:
Step 1: Draw the base AB = 5 cm using a ruler.
Step 2: At point A, construct an angle of 45° and draw a line extending from A.
Step 3: At point B, construct an angle of 80° and draw a line extending from B.
Step 4: The point where these two lines intersect is vertex C.
Step 5: The triangle ABC is now complete.
However, not all combinations of two angles and a side produce a triangle. The two angles must satisfy a special condition.

topics.topic_name: Condition for Triangle Existence with Two Angles

content.title: Sum of Two Angles Rule
content.text: For a triangle to exist with two given angles and an included side, the sum of the two angles must be less than 180°. Mathematically: ∠A + ∠B < 180°.

content.title: Testing Angle Combinations
content.text:
Example 1: ∠A = 45°, ∠B = 80°. Sum = 125° < 180°. Triangle exists. The third angle is 180° - 125° = 55°.

Example 2: ∠A = 150°, ∠B = 50°. Sum = 200° > 180°. Triangle does NOT exist because the two rays from A and B would be parallel or diverging, and they cannot meet.

The reason is geometric: if the sum of two angles is greater than or equal to 180°, the two line segments drawn from A and B at these angles will either be parallel or diverging, so they cannot meet to form a closed triangle.

content.title: The Angle Sum Property
content.text: The sum of all three angles in any triangle is always 180°. Mathematically: ∠A + ∠B + ∠C = 180°
This remarkable property holds for all triangles, regardless of their shape or size. It is one of the most fundamental properties in geometry.

content.title: Proof Using Parallel Lines
content.text:
Consider triangle ABC. Construct a line XY through vertex A that is parallel to BC.

Since XY is parallel to BC:
- ∠XAB = ∠B (alternate angles with transversal AB)
- ∠YAC = ∠C (alternate angles with transversal AC)

On the straight line XY, the angles form 180°:
∠XAB + ∠BAC + ∠YAC = 180°

Substituting the equal angles:
∠B + ∠A + ∠C = 180°

This proves the angle sum property!

content.title: Finding an Unknown Angle
content.text:
In a triangle, if two angles are 50° and 60°, find the third angle.
Using the angle sum property:
50° + 60° + third angle = 180°
110° + third angle = 180°
Third angle = 70°
An exterior angle of a triangle is formed when one side of the triangle is extended beyond a vertex. The angle between this extended line and the adjacent side is the exterior angle.

content.title: Finding an Exterior Angle
content.text:
In triangle ABC, if we extend side BC beyond C, the angle ACD formed is an exterior angle.

If ∠A = 50° and ∠B = 60°, find the exterior angle ∠ACD.

First, find ∠C using angle sum property:
∠C = 180° - 50° - 60° = 70°

Since ∠ACD and ∠ACB form a linear pair (straight angle):
∠ACD = 180° - ∠ACB = 180° - 70° = 110°

content.title: Exterior Angle Theorem
content.text: An exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles. Mathematically: Exterior angle = sum of two remote interior angles.
Using this property, ∠ACD = ∠A + ∠B = 50° + 60° = 110°, which matches our calculation above.

An altitude of a triangle is a perpendicular line segment drawn from a vertex to the opposite side (or the line containing the opposite side). The length of this altitude is referred to as the height of the triangle with respect to that base.

content.title: Key Points About Altitudes
content.text:
- Every triangle has three altitudes, one from each vertex.
- An altitude is perpendicular (forms a 90° angle) with the base.
- The altitude may lie outside the triangle if the triangle is obtuse.

content.title: Altitudes in Different Triangles
content.text:
In an acute triangle: All three altitudes lie inside the triangle.

In a right triangle: The altitude from the right angle vertex to the hypotenuse lies inside. The two sides meeting at the right angle are themselves altitudes.

In an obtuse triangle: The altitudes from the acute angle vertices lie outside the triangle, meeting the extended lines of the opposite sides.
Constructing an altitude precisely requires ensuring a 90° angle. While a ruler alone could be used, it would require many trials. The most efficient method uses a set square.

content.title: Constructing an Altitude Using a Set Square
content.text:
Step 1: Place the ruler along the base of the triangle.
Step 2: Position the set square on the ruler so one edge of the right angle aligns with the ruler.
Step 3: Slide the set square along the ruler until its vertical edge passes through the opposite vertex.
Step 4: Draw a line along the vertical edge of the set square. This is the altitude—it is perpendicular to the base.
A right-angled triangle (or right triangle) is a triangle with one angle equal to 90°. This special property creates a unique relationship between the sides and altitudes.

content.title: Special Property
content.text: In a right-angled triangle, the two sides forming the right angle are themselves altitudes to each other. No separate altitude construction is needed for these two sides.

content.title: Altitudes in Right Triangle ABC with Right Angle at B
content.text:
In triangle ABC with ∠B = 90°:
- The altitude from A is along side AB (perpendicular to BC).
- The altitude from C is along side BC (perpendicular to AB).
- The altitude from B is perpendicular to AC (the hypotenuse).

content.title: Three Types Based on Sides
items: Equilateral Triangle: All three sides are equal. All three angles are also equal (each 60°).
Isosceles Triangle: Two sides are equal. The angles opposite the equal sides are also equal.
Scalene Triangle: All three sides have different lengths. All three angles have different measures.

content.text: The classification by sides reflects the symmetry of the triangle. The more sides are equal, the more symmetric the triangle.

content.title: Three Types Based on Angles
items: Acute-Angled Triangle: All three angles are acute angles (less than 90°).
Right-Angled Triangle: One angle is exactly 90° (a right angle).
Obtuse-Angled Triangle: One angle is obtuse (greater than 90° but less than 180°).

content.title: Important Distinction
content.text: An acute-angled triangle is NOT simply a triangle with one acute angle. It must have ALL three angles as acute angles. This is because if one angle is 90° or more, the other two must together sum to 90° or less, making it impossible for all three to be acute.

content.title: Examples of Each Type
content.text:
Acute triangle: angles 60°, 70°, 50° (all less than 90°)
Right triangle: angles 90°, 50°, 40° (one angle is 90°)
Obtuse triangle: angles 100°, 50°, 30° (one angle is more than 90°)

topics.topic_name: Combining Classification by Sides and Angles

content.text: A triangle can be classified in both ways simultaneously. For example, a triangle can be both isosceles and acute-angled, or scalene and right-angled.

content.title: Combined Classification
content.text:
Isosceles right triangle: Two equal sides, with the angle between them being 90°. The two base angles are each 45°.

Equilateral acute triangle: All sides equal, all angles 60° (which are all acute). This is the only possible type for equilateral triangles.

Can an equilateral triangle be right-angled? No, because in an equilateral triangle, each angle is 60°, not 90°.

glossary.definition: A polygon with three sides, three vertices, and three angles; denoted as ∆ABC with vertices A, B, and C.
A corner point of a triangle where two sides meet; every triangle has three vertices.
A triangle in which all three sides are of equal length and all three angles are equal (each 60°).
A triangle in which two sides are of equal length; the angles opposite the equal sides are also equal.
A triangle in which all three sides have different lengths and all three angles have different measures.
The condition that for three lengths to form a triangle, each length must be smaller than the sum of the other two.
A method of constructing triangles using two known side lengths and the angle between them (the included angle).
A method of constructing triangles using two known angles and the side between them (the included side).
The property that the sum of all three angles in any triangle is always 180°.
An angle formed between an extended side of a triangle and an adjacent side; equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles.
A perpendicular line segment drawn from a vertex of a triangle to the opposite side (or its extension); represents the height of the triangle.
A triangle with one angle equal to 90°; the side opposite the right angle is called the hypotenuse.
A triangle in which all three angles are acute (less than 90°).
A triangle in which one angle is obtuse (greater than 90° but less than 180°).
The angle between two known sides in a triangle; used in SAS construction method.
The side between two known angles in a triangle; used in ASA construction method.
A drawing tool used to construct arcs and circles with a fixed radius, essential for efficient triangle construction.
A right-angled triangular tool used to construct perpendicular lines and angles like 90°, 45°, and 60°.

summary_points: A triangle is a closed shape with three vertices (corners), three sides, and three angles; triangles are named using their vertices (∆ABC) and can have various shapes and sizes.
The triangle inequality states that for three lengths to form a triangle, each length must be smaller than the sum of the other two; if satisfied, a triangle exists; if not, it cannot exist.

Triangles can be efficiently constructed using a compass when given: (a) all three side lengths, (b) two sides and the included angle (SAS method), or (c) two angles and the included side (ASA method).
The sum of all three angles in any triangle is always 180° (angle sum property); this fundamental property holds for all triangles and can be proven using parallel lines and alternate angles.
For two angles and an included side to form a triangle, the sum of those two angles must be less than 180°; if the sum is ≥180°, no triangle can be formed.
An exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent (remote) interior angles; exterior angles are formed by extending a side of the triangle.

Triangles are classified by sides as equilateral (all sides equal), isosceles (two sides equal), or scalene (all sides different); and by angles as acute (all angles <90°), right (one angle =90°), or obtuse (one angle >90°).
An altitude of a triangle is a perpendicular from a vertex to the opposite side; every triangle has three altitudes, and their length represents the height of the triangle with respect to that base.

In a right-angled triangle, the two sides forming the 90° angle serve as altitudes to each other; in obtuse triangles, some altitudes lie outside the triangle, meeting extended lines of opposite sides.

An equilateral triangle has all sides equal, all angles equal to 60°, and high symmetry; an isosceles triangle has two equal sides and equal angles opposite these sides; a scalene triangle has no equal sides or angles.
